title: Fourier Analysis: An Introduction
excerpt: The first volume of the celebrated Princeton Lectures in Analysis, presenting Fourier series, Fourier transforms, and finite Fourier analysis as a unified subject rooted in physical intuition and rigorous analysis.
whoShouldRead: Advanced undergraduates and beginning graduate students in mathematics
Physics and engineering students wanting rigorous foundations
Anyone planning to read the full Princeton Lectures in Analysis series
whoShouldSkip: Students without a solid background in undergraduate real analysis
keyIdeas: Arbitrary functions can be represented as infinite sums of trigonometric functions
Convergence of Fourier series requires careful analysis of kernels and summability methods
The Fourier transform extends Fourier analysis from periodic to non-periodic functions on R and R^d
Finite Fourier analysis on abelian groups connects to number theory via Dirichlet's theorem
The whole of analysis is a unified subject
keyTakeaways: Fourier analysis emerged from the physical problems of the vibrating string and heat diffusion
Cesàro and Abel summability provide convergence where pointwise convergence fails
The Fourier transform is a powerful tool for solving partial differential equations
Finite Fourier analysis on Z(N) leads to the fast Fourier transform and deep number theory
